figures.caption: Figure 6: (A) Monoamine-connectome prediction (F1) versus lag for SBTG and representative baselines, shown separately by transmitter (dopamine, serotonin, tyramine, octopamine).(B) Celltype coupling heatmaps (sensory/interneuron/motor) as a function of lag, comparing SBTG to VAR and Pearson. (C) Randi functional benchmark performance versus lag (AUROC, AUPRC) across methods.

Figure 1: Goal: inferring connections from activity. A: C. elegans (a small worm) with cartoon anatomical connectome. B: Available data: neural activity measurements for (3) sampled neurons. The activity window shows observed ”state” of brain at time t. C: SBTG algorithm; see Figure 2. D: Inferred putative functional connectome; i.e., which connections among neurons likely caused the state to evolve. E: Verification of putative connectome against anatomy.
Table 5: Hyperparameter search space for Optuna optimization.

Figure 7: Lag-resolved recoverability for structural and receptor networks. Top: Chemical vs. Gap Junction benchmarks as a function of lag (left: AUPRC, right: F1). The Gap Junction network shows a sharp peak at t = 0.25s and rapidly decays, whereas Chemical Synapses exhibit broader persistence across lags. Bottom: GABA-A vs. GABA-B benchmarks (left: AUPRC, right: F1), showing strongest recoverability at the shortest lag with additional lag-dependent structure, particularly for GABA-B.

Figure 3: Stimulus Phase Analysis. A: Stimulus phase segmentation used for analysis (baseline, on, steady, off). B: Number of FDR-significant directed edges by lag within each phase. C: Cell-type coupling strength versus lag within each phase, summarizing directed interactions between sensory (S), interneuron (I), and motor (M) groups.
